title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a scientist called Ryan Miller talking about the potential of fungi in the modern world. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a scientist called Ryan Miller talking about the potential of fungi in the modern world. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Ryan Miller explores how mushrooms and mycelium can solve environmental and industrial problems.
extracts.audio_script:
Hi, I’m Ryan. For a long time, fungi were considered a nuisance—something that ruins your bread or grows in the corners of a damp house. But to a mycologist, they are the world’s most advanced chemical engineers. My interest in this kingdom didn't start in a lab; it actually began when I found a [7]giant puffball mushroom[/7] in the woods behind my house as a boy. I was amazed at how it could grow from nothing to the size of a football in just a few days. I wanted to understand that explosive growth.

We are now discovering that fungi can help us clean up the planet. This process is called 'mycoremediation'. I’ve found that certain species can actually [8]break down oil[/8] spills in the soil. They produce enzymes that digest complex hydrocarbons and turn them into harmless organic matter. It’s a completely natural way to heal land that has been contaminated by industry. It’s cheaper and more effective than many of our traditional chemical treatments.

Fungi are also changing the construction industry. We are now 'growing' bricks using mycelium—the root-like structure of a fungus. I noticed that [9]agricultural waste[/9], like corn husks or sawdust, is the perfect fuel for this process. We put the waste into a mold, add the mycelium, and in a few days, it becomes a solid, fire-resistant block. It’s a zero-waste material that could eventually replace plastic and even some types of concrete.

In the world of medicine, we are only scratching the surface. I’ve found that [10]immune system regulation[/10] is the most promising area of research. Many mushrooms contain compounds that can help our bodies recognize and fight diseases more effectively. We are studying how these can be used to supplement modern treatments for chronic illnesses. It’s a return to ancient wisdom through a scientific lens.

Technology is helping us map the 'Wood Wide Web'. This is the underground network of fungi that allows trees to communicate with each other. I noticed that [11]nutrient exchange[/11] between different species of plants is managed by these fungal networks. If a tree is dying, it can send its remaining resources to younger trees through the mycelium. It’s a level of cooperation that we never thought possible in nature.

However, we have to protect these networks. I’m worried that [12]intensive farming[/12] is destroying the soil’s fungal diversity. When we use too many pesticides or turn the soil over too often, we break the connections that keep the forest healthy. We need to rethink how we manage our land before we lose these invisible support systems.

Looking forward, I believe we will see 'living clothes' made from fungi. I noticed that [13]biodegradable leather[/13] grown from mycelium is already being used by major fashion brands. It feels like real leather but can be composted at the end of its life. It’s the ultimate sustainable solution for the fashion industry, which is currently one of the world’s biggest polluters.

Finally, I’m currently developing a [14]field identification app[/14] that uses AI to help amateur foragers identify mushrooms safely. I want to encourage people to explore the world of fungi without the risk of accidental poisoning. Thanks for listening.

dialogue.speaker: ryan

questions.question: Ryan’s interest in mushrooms was triggered by finding a (7) near his home.
questions.explanation: He mentions it 'began when I found a giant puffball mushroom in the woods'.

questions.question: Ryan explains that some fungi have the ability to (8) in polluted areas.
questions.explanation: He found that 'certain species can actually break down oil spills in the soil'.

questions.question: Ryan says that (9) is used as the base for creating environmentally-friendly bricks.
questions.explanation: He noticed that 'agricultural waste... is the perfect fuel for this process'.

questions.question: According to Ryan, (10) is the most significant health benefit currently being studied.
questions.explanation: He's found that 'immune system regulation is the most promising area of research'.

questions.question: Ryan explains that fungi facilitate the (11) between different trees in a forest.
questions.explanation: He noticed that 'nutrient exchange between different species of plants is managed by these fungal networks'.

questions.question: Ryan is concerned that (12) is putting the health of underground fungal networks at risk.
questions.explanation: He is worried that 'intensive farming is destroying the soil’s fungal diversity'.

questions.question: Ryan points out that (13) is an eco-friendly material that is already being used in fashion.
